# The peroxidase/catalase-like activities of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni, Cu) MNPs and their application in colorimetric biosensing of glucose


Li Su a,b , Wenjie Qin a,c , Huige Zhang a,c , Zia Ur Rahman a,c , Cuiling Ren a,c , Sudai Ma a,c , Xingguo Chen a,c, n

- a State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou730000, China

b School of Chemistry and Chemistry Engineering, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, Henan453007, China

c Department of Chemistry, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou730000, China


# a r t i c l e i n f o


Article history: Received17May2014Received in revised form15July2014Accepted22July2014

Available online25July2014


# Keywords:


MFe2O4magnetic nanoparticles Peroxidase/catalase mimetic Colorimetric biosensing Glucose detection


# 1. Introduction


Artificial enzyme mimics have been the focus of intensive research because of many disadvantages in the application of natural enzymes such as easy denaturation by environmental changes and digestion by proteases (Shoji and Freund, 2001). Furthermore, the preparation, purification and storage of natural enzymes are usually time-consuming and expensive, which limit their widespread applications (Breslow, 1995). At present, a large number of artificial enzymes have been constructed by incorporating a catalytic center into a variety of scaffolds to mimic natural enzymes, including ligand-anchoring supramolecular complexes, micelles, vesicles, nanoparticles, nanotubes and nanogels (Deuss et al., 2011; Dong et al., 2012, 2011; Murakami et al., 1996; Wiester et al., 2011).

The nanomaterial-based artificial enzymes (nanozymes) have been concerned because that their preparation, storage, and separation are relatively simple and cheaply. And they have already found wide applications in numerousfields, including biosensing, immunoassays, cancer diagnostics and therapy, neuroprotection, stem cell growth, and pollutant removal (Wei and Wang, 2013). Recently, Since Fe3O4magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs)

n Corresponding author. Tel.: þ 86 931 8912763; fax: þ 86 931 8912582. E-mail address: chenxg@lzu.edu.cn (X. Chen).


# a b s t r a c t


MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni, Cu) magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) were found to have catalytic activities similar to those of biological enzymes such as catalase and peroxidase. These nanomaterials, as bifunctional catalase/peroxidases (KatGs), not only could catalyze H2O2to produce hydroxyl radicals, which oxidized peroxidase substrate to produce color, but also could catalyze the decomposition reaction of H2O2into water and oxygen directly in the same condition through the catalase-like activity. And it was also found that the amount of generated hydroxyl radicals and oxygen was related to the concentration of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni, Cu) MNPs. The peroxidase-like catalytic behavior of MFe2O4MNPs was analyzed in detail. Under the optimized conditions, NiFe2O4MNPs were used as a colorimetric biosensor for the detection of9.4 /C2 10 /C0 7 -2.5 /C2 10 /C0 5mol L /C0 1glucose with a limit of detection (LOD) of4.5 /C2 10 /C0 7mol L /C0 1 . The sensor was successfully applied to glucose detection in urine sample.

& 2014Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

was found to possess intrinsic peroxidase mimetic activity (Gao et al., 2007), a variety of nanomaterials including metal oxide (Andre et al., 2011; Chen et al., 2011; Mu et al., 2012), carbon materials (Cui et al., 2011; Song et al., 2010; Zheng et al., 2013), noble metal (Jiang et al., 2012; Jv et al., 2010) and nanocomposites (Bi et al., 2014; Xing et al., 2014) displayed peroxidase-like mimetic activity. The intrinsic enzyme-like activity of nanomaterials has become a growing area of interest. Therefore, it is crucial to look for high catalytic efficiency, stable, rapid separated peroxidase mimetic from the view point of practical application.

The cubic spinel structured MFe2O4is an important class of iron oxide materials, in which oxygen forms a face-centered cubic (fcc) close packing. M2 þ and half of Fe3 þ ions occupy the octahedral interstitial sites, while the other half occupy the tetrahedral sites. Divalent metal ions take the place of Fe2 þ of Fe3O4to synthesize MFe2O4compounds, which show a perfect resistance to the oxidation of Fe2 þ in air atmosphere (Cabrera et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2011; Pazik et al., 2013). MFe2O4MNPs have been in the forefront of nanoscience and nanotechnology because of their outstanding properties such as nanometer size, large surface area to volume ratio, superparamagnetic behavior, and high saturation magnetization (Jeong et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2007; Veiseh et al., 2010). This class of nanomaterials has been conquering new horizons in numerous researchfields including biomedicine (Xie et al., 2011; Yoo et al., 2011), drug delivery (Kumar and

Mohammad, 2011), environmental remediation (Jung et al., 2011), catalysis (Polshettiwar et al., 2011), magnetic resonance image (MRI) (Frey et al., 2009) and so on. Very recently, great effort has been made to provide new insights into the application of MFe2O4in sensing. Moreover, ferrite nanoparticles such as CoFe2O4MNPs (Shi et al., 2011), BiFeO3MNPs (Luo et al., 2010), MnFe2O4 (Figueroa-Espí et al., 2011), and ZnFe2O4MNPs (Su et al., 2012) have been reported to show intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity for colorimetric biosensing.

In this work, the dual enzyme-like activity of the generic MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni, Cu) MNPs has been studied. MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni, Cu) MNPs were found to be able to exhibit catalase/peroxidase-like (KatGs-like) activities. To the best of our knowledge, such dual enzyme-like catalytic activities of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni, Cu) MNPs have not been reported. As mimic catalase, MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni, Cu) MNPs could catalyze the decomposition reaction of H2O2into water and oxygen. The peroxidase-like activity of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni, Cu) MNPs were tested with2.2-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6sulfonic acid) (ABTS) as the substrate. And the mechanisms of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni, Cu) MNPs of KatGs-like activities were discussed. In addition, MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni, Cu) MNPs, as mimic peroxidase, possessed some advantage including easy preparation, stability, repeatability, dispersibility, and rapid separation. We have employed NiFe2O4MNPs as a model to offer a simple and selective colorimetric method for glucose determination in urine sample.


# 2. Experimental section



# 2.1. Materials


30% H2O2, CuCl2 /C1 2H2O, NiCl2 /C1 6H2O, CuCl2 /C1 6H2O, FeCl3 /C1 6H2O, ethylene glycol, polyethylene glycol, terephthalic acid, NaCH2COOH (NaAc), and CH3COOH (HAc) were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). 2.2 ′ -azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) and3.3 ′ ,5.5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Glucose was purchased from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai) Co. Ltd. Glucose oxidase (GOx, 50KU) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and stored in a refrigerator at /C0 18 ° C.

All other chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. Deionized water was used throughout the experiment.


# 2.2. Apparatus and characterization


A TU-1901double beams UV -vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkine General Instrument Co. Ltd., China) was used to implement kinetic experiments and scan spectrum. A722S spectrophotometer (Shanghai Precision & Scientific Instrument Co. Ltd.) was used to measure absorbance. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni, Cu) MNPs were obtained using a Tecnai G2F30instrument. The surface morphology of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni, Cu) MNPs was taken on afield emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM Hitachi S-4800, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni, Cu) MNPs was carried out with X'Pertpro Philips X-ray diffractometer with Cu K α radiation ( λ ¼ 0.154056). Magnetic hysteresis loop of the prepared MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni, Cu) MNPs was carried out with a vibration sample magnetometer (VSM, Lakeshore cryotronics730, USA) at room temperature. Fluorometric measurement was carried out by RF-5301PC spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu). Element determination was measured by a4300DV inductively coupled plasma -atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES, PerkinElmer, USA). Zeta potential measurements of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni, Cu) MNPs were performed with a Malvern Nano-ZS apparatus. A

DW1Oxygen Electrode Chamber (Hansatech Instruments Ltd.) was used to detect dissolved oxygen.


# 2.3. Preparation of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni, Cu) MNPs


MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni, Cu) MNPs were prepared using a hydrothermal method as described elsewhere (Deng et al., 2007, 2005). Briefly, the MgFe2O4MNPs were prepared as follows: MgCl2 /C1 6H2O (2.5mmol) and FeCl3 /C1 6H2O (5mmol) were dissolved in ethylene glycol (40mL) to form a clear solution, followed by the addition of NaAc (3.6g) and polyethylene glycol20000 (1.0g). The mixture was stirred vigorously until the reactants dissolved, and then sealed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (50mL capacity). The autoclave was heated to and maintained at200 ° C for6h, and allowed to cool to room temperature. The black products were washed several times with ethanol and deionized water, and dried at60 ° C for6h.

The synthesis of CuFe2O4MNPs is similar to that of MgFe2O4MNPs. Specifically, CuCl2 /C1 2H2O (2.5mmol) and FeCl3 /C1 6H2O (5mmol) were dissolved in ethylene glycol (40mL) to form a clear solution, followed by the addition of NaAc (3.6g) and polyethylene glycol20000 (1.0g). The dissolved reactants were sealed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel50mL autoclave and maintained at200 ° C for8h, and allowed to cool to room temperature. The products were washed several times with ethanol and deionized water, and dried at60 ° C for6h.

The NiFe2O4MNPs were prepared according to the major part of the above described process. NiCl2 /C1 2H2O (2.5mmol) and FeCl3 /C1 6H2O (5mmol) were dissolved in ethylene glycol (40mL) to form a clear solution, followed by the addition of NaAc (3.6g) and polyethylene glycol600 (2mL). The dissolved reactants were sealed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel50mL autoclave and maintained at200 ° C for18h, and allowed to cool to room temperature. The products were washed several times with ethanol and deionized water, and dried at60 ° C for6h.


# 2.4. Kinetic analysis


Kinetic experiments were carried out at45 ° C using40 μ g mL /C0 1MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni) MNPs in a reaction volume of3.0mL HAc -NaAc buffer solution (0.2M, pH4.0) with ABTS or TMB as substrate, and the concentration of H2O2wasfixed, unless otherwise stated. Kinetic measurements were carried out in time course mode by monitoring the absorbance change at417or652nm for4min on TU-1901UV -vis spectrophotometer. The Michaelis -Menten constant was calculated using a Lineweaver -Burk plot:

where ν is the initial velocity, Vmax represents the maximal reaction velocity, and [ S ] is the substrate concentration. Km is Michaelis -Menten constant, which is an indicator of enzyme affinity for its substrate. The smaller the value of Km is, the stronger the affinity between the enzyme and the substrate.


# 2.5. OH radical and dissolved O2measurement


50mM H2O2, 0.625mM terephthalic acid and different concentrations of the MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni) MNPs werefirst incubated in0.2M HAc -NaAc buffer (pH4.0) at45 ° C for8h. The MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni) MNPs were then removed from the reaction solution by an external magneticfield. The part of the above solutions were used forfluorometric measurement OH radical by RF-5301PC spectrofluorophotometer. Meanwhile, the other part of the above solutions was used for dissolved oxygen detection by the DW1Oxygen Electrode Chamber.


# 2.6. Detection of glucose using NiFe2O4MNPs and GOx


Glucose detection was performed as follows: a) GOx (10 μ L1mg mL /C0 1 ) and45 μ L of different concentration of glucose in0.5mM Na2HPO4buffer (pH7.0) were incubated at37 ° C for30min; b) ABTS (55 μ L30mM), NiFe2O4MNPs (20 μ L5mg mL /C0 1 ), and470 μ L of0.2M HAc -NaAc buffer (pH4.0) were added into the above55 μ L incubated reaction, and pH of thefinal resulting solution is4.0; c) the mixed solution was incubated at45 ° C for10min and then kept in an ice -water bath for10min to terminate the reaction; d) the NiFe2O4MNPs were then removed from the reaction solution by an external magneticfield; e) 500 μ L of the resulting solution without NiFe2O4MNPs was added into2.5mL deionized water in a4.0mL centrifuge tube, then the solution was mixed up to make it uniform and used for absorbance measurement at wavelength417nm.

For glucose determination in urine from diabetics, the collected samples were stored at /C0 18 ° C and thawed at ambient temperature before further preparation. Firstly, the samples were centrifuged at12000rpm for40min, and then the supernatants were diluted20times using NaH2PO4buffer (0.5mM, pH7.0) before measurement.


# 3. Results and discussion



# 3.1. Characterization of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Cu, Ni) MNPs


The crystallinity of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Cu, Ni) MNPs were examined by the X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. As shown in Fig. S1a, the diffraction patterns are indexed to be MgFe2O4 (JCPDS73-2410). Except Cu impurity peak, all peaks in Fig. S1b are indexed to be CuFe2O4 (JCPDS77-0010). Likewise, except some Ni peaks, all other peaks in Fig. S1c are indexed to be NiFe2O4 (JCPDS86-2267). These results are agreement with that of the literature (Deng et al., 2007).

The surface morphologies of the as-synthesized MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Cu, Ni) MNPs were investigated byfield emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM). Fig. S2presents the corresponding FESEM images of the products in which MgFe2O4MNPs exhibits spherical shapes with smooth surfaces. On the other hand, CuFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs display irregular spherical-like shapes with rough surfaces.

To provide additional insight into the structure of the asprepared nanomaterials, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analyses were carried out. Typical TEM image in Fig. S3a illustrates that the MgFe2O4MNPs are regular spherical with average size of about220nm. On the other hand, the average size of CuFe2O4MNPs composited of loose small nanoparticles is about150nm. Meanwhile, NiFe2O4MNPs are highly irregular spherical with average size of about200nm.

Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis was also used to determine the chemical composition of the as-prepared ferrite MNPs products. Results from EDX spectra of prepared ferrite microspheres show that samples contain Mg, Fe, O for MgFe2O4MNPs (Fig. S4a), Cu, Fe, O for CuFe2O4MNPs (Fig. S4b), and Ni, Fe, O for NiFe2O4MNPs (Fig. S4c). The results are consistent with that of ICP-AES (data not given).

Fig. S5shows that the magnetization hysteresis loops appeared S-like and the saturation magnetization of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Cu, Ni) MNPs. The magnetic saturation values are69.5emu g /C0 1for MgFe2O4MNPs, 50.0emu g /C0 1for CuFe2O4MNPs and43.0emu g /C0 1for NiFe2O4MNPs.

Fig. 1. Peroxidase-like activity and catalase-like activity of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Cu, Ni) MNPs (a, b). (a) Photograph of color reaction systems: (1) MgFe2O4MNPs þ ABTS, (2) NiFe2O4MNPs þ ABTS, (3) CuFe2O4MNPs þ ABTS, (4) ABTS þ H2O2, (5)  ABTS þ MgFe2O4MNPs þ H2O2, (6) ABTS þ NiFe2O4MNPs þ H2O2, and (7)  ABTS þ CuFe2O4MNPs þ H2O2in a pH4.0HAc -NaAc buffer (0.2M) at45 ° C for10min. (b) Photograph of bubble reaction systems: (1) H2O2, (2) H2O2 þ NiFe2O4MNPs, (3) H2O2 þ MgFe2O4MNP,(4) H2O2 þ CuFe2O4MNPs in a pH4.0HAc -NaAc buffer (0.2M) at45 ° C for6h. (For interpretation of the references to color in thisfigure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)


# 3.2. Dual enzyme-like activity of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Cu, Ni) MNPs


The catalysis of peroxidase substrate2.2 ′ -azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) was tested in the presence of H2O2. MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Cu, Ni) MNPs were added into substrate ABTS in the presence of H2O2. As shown in Fig. 1a, MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Cu, Ni) MNPs could catalyze the oxidation of ABTS by H2O2to produce the typical green color. Additional control experiments showed that the green color of the MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Cu, Ni) MNPs -ABTS -H2O2system was much deeper than that of the ABTS -H2O2system, and the MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Cu, Ni) MNPs -ABTS system did not produce typical green color. It indicates that MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Cu, Ni) MNPs possessed peroxidase-like activity. More significantly, we found that MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Cu, Ni) MNPs exhibited intrinsic catalase-like activity in the same condition. Gas bubbles were observed several minutes after H2O2was added into tubes containing MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Cu, Ni) MNPs diluted with pH4.0buffer and gradually grew bigger in the following hours (Fig. 1b), indicating that MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Cu, Ni) MNPs might be have similarly as catalase to decompose H2O2into water and oxygen.


# 3.3. Leaching effect of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Cu, Ni) MNPs


The effect of pH on peroxidase-like activity of the MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Cu, Ni) MNPs was studied in the range of pH3 -7. The results in Fig. 2a indicated that the catalytic activity was much higher in acidic solutions. However, the iron ions could be leached from the MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Cu, Ni) MNPs in the acidic buffer solutions. The amount of leached iron ions was determined by ICP-OES. As shown in Fig. 2b, the iron ions indeed were leached

Fig. 2. (a) A pH dependent response curve for the MFe2O4MNPs -ABTS -H2O2system incubated at45 ° C. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements. The maximum point in each curve was set as100%. (b) The pH dependence of the Fe ions leaching from the as prepared MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni, Cu) MNPs. (c) The response curve of the catalysis reaction of ABTS and H2O2using the leaching solution from the CuFe2O4MNPs, NiFe2O4MNPs, MgFe2O4MNPs and blank in different pH solutions.

from the MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Cu, Ni) MNPs in an acid solution, especially CuFe2O4MNPs. The amount of iron ions leaching from CuFe2O4MNPs is10 -200mg L /C0 1in the pH4.5 -3.0. And the amount of iron ions leaching from MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs is less than1mg L /C0 1in the pH for4.0. Meanwhile, the catalytic activity of the leached iron ions was further tested. As shown in Fig. 2c, the catalytic activity of the iron ions leaching from CuFe2O4MNPs could be detected in pH r5.0. However, when the NiFe2O4and MgFe2O4MNPs incubated in pH Z4.0buffer solutions, nearly no catalytic activity could be detected. The results indicated that the as-prepared NiFe2O4and MgFe2O4MNPs were stable in pH Z4.0buffer solutions. In view of the stability and catalytic activity of the catalyst, pH ¼ 4.0was set as the optimal value.


# 3.4. The dependence of the peroxidase-like activity of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni) MNPs


The reaction temperature-dependent response curves were shown in Fig. S6. From Fig. S6a, the signals increased as the reaction temperature increased. When the temperature exceeded45 ° C, the signals increased slower than that of the temperature below45 ° C. Therefore, 45 ° C was taken as the optimal reaction temperature. As shown in Fig. S6b, the optimal concentration of

H2O2was0.013mol L /C0 1for MgFe2O4MNPs and0.007mol L /C0 1for NiFe2O4MNPs.


# 3.5. Peroxidase activity parameter of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni) MNPs


To investigate the mechanism of the peroxidase-like activity of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni) MNPs, the apparent steady-state kinetic parameters for the reaction were determined by changing the concentration of peroxidase substrate (ABTS or TMB) and H2O2in this system, respectively. ABTS and TMB, as chromogenic substrate, were chosen because they possess opposite charge characteristics. ABTS contains negatively charged sulfonic groups, giving it a strong electrostatic affinity toward the positively charged nanoparticles. Conversely, TMB contains two amine groups, yielding a strong affinity toward the negatively charged nanoparticles. Experimental data were in strong support of these claims, as demonstrated by the calculated catalytic parameters shown in Table1. MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs yielded lower Km values toward ABTS compared with TMB, which suggested positively charged MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs showed a strong electrostatic affinity towards ABTS. Comparing the affinity of MgFe2O4MNPs and NiFe2O4MNPs toward ABTS and TMB, it was found that MgFe2O4MNPs had a higher affinity towards ABTS than

'-' data not provided in literature.

NiFe2O4MNPs, and the opposite occurred for TMB. That is because that the higher value of zeta potential of MgFe2O4MNPs than NiFe2O4MNPs lead to the stronger electrostatic affinity toward negatively charged ABTS. As the results, ABTS was chosen in order to enhance the affinity of MNPs with peroxidase substrate, which results in a significantly improved peroxidase activity. In addition, the apparent Km value for MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs with H2O2as the substrate was larger than HRP (Table1), suggesting that MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs had lower affinity for H2O2than HRP. The Km value of MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs with ABTS was lower than that of HRP, suggesting that MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs had a higher affinity for ABTS than HRP.

To further investigate the mechanism of the catalysis of MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs, their activity over a range of ABTS and H2O2concentrations were measured. The double reciprocal plots of initial velocity against the concentration of one substrate were obtained over a range of concentrations of the second substrate (Fig. S8a -d). The slopes of the lines were parallel, which was characteristic of a ping-pong mechanism. This indicated that MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs bind and react with thefirst substrate and then release thefirst product before reacting with the second substrate.


# 3.6. Robustness and repeatability of peroxidase activity of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni) MNPs


The stability of MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs in wide pH and temperature ranges is crucial to extend their applications. As inorganic materials, MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs ware expected to be more stable than natural enzymes. As for HRP, after treatment at pH lower than5.0or temperatures greater than40 ° C for2h, the enzyme activity dramatically declines (Gao et al., 2007). However, the peroxidase activity of MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs were stable when they were incubated at a wide range of pH (3 -11) and temperatures (0 -90 ° C) for2h (Fig. S9). In addition,

Fig. 3. The effect of (a) MgFe2O4MNPs and (b) NiFe2O4MNPs on the formation of hydroxyl radical with terephthalic acid as afluorescence probe. The effect of (c) MgFe2O4MNPs and (d) NiFe2O4MNPs on the generation of dissolved O2.

in view of the repeatability of peroxidase activity of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni) MNPs, the batch-to-batch reproducibility and longterm repeatability were researched. The repeatability of peroxidase activity was evaluated using the relative standard deviation (RSD) of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni) MNPs catalyzing the reaction of ABTS and H2O2. From Fig. S10, the value of RSD was4.4% and3.9% for three batches MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs, respectively. And the value of RSD was5.15% and3.41% for long-term storage MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs, respectively. The above results illustrated that the peroxidase activity of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni) MNPs could be reproducible. The robustness and repeatability of NiFe2O4and MgFe2O4MNPs makes them suitable for a broad range of applications in the biomedicine and environmental chemistryfields.


# 3.7. Possible mechanism of dual enzyme-like activity of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni) MNPs


The possible mechanism of peroxidase activity are that MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni) MNPs can catalyzed H2O2to produce d OH, which oxidize peroxidase substrate (ABTS and TMB) to produce color. The d OH is assessed by adding thefluorescent probe terephthalic acid into the H2O2 -MgFe2O4/NiFe2O4MNPs system, where terephthalic acid easily reacts with d OH to form highlyfluorescent2-hydroxy terephthalic acid (Ishibashi et al., 2000). As shown in Fig. 3a and b, thefluorescence intensity was weak when there were no MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs. Nevertheless, the increase of thefluorescence intensity was observed when the concentration of MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs increased to1mg mL /C0 1and0.36mg mL /C0 1 , respectively, which suggested that MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs catalyzed H2O2to produce d OH. However, the decrease of thefluorescence intensity was observed as the concentration of MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs continue to increase. This supports the theory that MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs possess catalase-like activity. This kind of activity has also been reported in some other nanomaterials, such as Au NPs (He et al., 2013), Au@PtNPs (He et al., 2011) and ferritin -platinum NPs (Fan et al., 2011). Meanwhile, MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni) MNPs also can catalyze H2O2to produce O2 (Fig. 3c and d), which was consistent with their catalase-like activities.

As shown in Fig. 3, the concentration of generated d OH and O2was connected with the amount of MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs. Then, the detailed process was presented in Scheme1.

H2O2molecules were adsorbed on the surface of MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs and then activated by the bound Fe3 þ to generate three intermediates (Fe2 þ , d OH and HO2d ) (shown in Reactions (2) -(3) and (4)) (Perez-Benito, 2004). That is, fi rst, at the low values of MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs (0.04 -0.2mg mL /C0 1 ), the concentrations of the three intermediates increase as the concentration of MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4increases, but those of Fe2 þ and d OH increase much more than that of HO2d (Perez-Benito, 2004).

And the predominant chain-termination step is expected to be Reaction (5). Hence, the rate of generated d OH increased much more that of O2 (Fig. 3). Second, at the moderate values of MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs (0.2 -1.0mg mL /C0 1 ), the concentration of d OH increased slowly (or decreased) with the concentration of O2increasing quickly (Fig. 3), which was due that hydroxyl radicals would immediately react with HO2d to produce oxygen (Reaction (6)) (Kwan and Voelker, 2002). Last, at the high values of MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs (1.0 -4.0mg mL /C0 1 ), Reaction (7) will be the predominant chain-termination step (Perez-Benito, 2004). Hence, the amount of d OH decreased and the concentration of O2reached the maximum and remained unchanged (Fig. 3)


# 3.8. Detection glucose using NiFe2O4MNPs


The glucose detection was performed in two separated steps as mentioned in the experimental section because GOx could be denatured at pH4.0buffer solution. Since the catalytic activity of NiFe2O4MNPs is the concentration of H2O2dependent, the H2O2produced in the glucose oxidation reaction by GOx was detected using NiFe2O4MNPs, as intrinsic peroxidase. Thus, colorimetric detection of glucose could be realized easily using our method. Fig. 4a shows typical glucose concentration -response curves. The linear range for glucose was from9.4 /C2 10 /C0 7to2.5 /C2 10 /C0 5mol L /C0 1with limit of detection (LOD) of4.5 /C2 10 /C0 7mol L /C0 1 (LOD ¼ KS0/ S , where K is a numerical factor chosen according to the confidence level desired, S0is the standard deviation (S.D.) of the blank measurements ( n ¼ 10, K ¼ 3) and S is the slope of calibration curve.) (Fig. 4b). This detection method based on the NiFe2O4MNPs -ABTS system gave a lower LOD than the method using Fe3O4MNPs -ABTS system (3 /C2 10 /C0 5mol L /C0 1 ) (Wei and Wang, 2008). Using this method, we detect glucose in urine samples with diabetes from male volunteers. According to the calibration curve, the concentration of glucose in urine was24.1mM, 11.7mM and4.90mM, respectively (Fig. 4c), which agrees well with that measured by the GOD-PAP biochemical analyzer in hospital (25.8mM, 11.3mM and5.20mM, respectively). The urine test is positive when the

Scheme1. Schematic illustration of the process of generated /C15OH and O2catalyzed H2O2by the different amount of MgFe2O4and NiFe2O4MNPs.

e

The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements. Inset: Photograph of the colored products for different concentrations of glucose ( μ M). amount of glucose is more than50 -100mg dL /C0 1 (Urakami et al., 2008). Therefore, this colorimetric method is applicable to determine glucose concentration in urine sample.

To test the selectivity of detection of glucose, the control experiments were taken using fructose, lactose, and maltose. The selectivity of the colorimetric method is shown in Fig. 4d. Even the concentration of control samples was5times larger than glucose, the color difference also can be distinguished by the naked eye. It shows that these compounds did not interfere with the determination of urine glucose.


# 4. Conclusion


In summary, MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni, Cu) MNPs were found to possess peroxidase-like activity as well as catalase-like activity. MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni, Cu) MNPs, as bifunctional catalase/peroxidases (KatGs), catalyzed H2O2to produce hydroxyl radicals and oxygen in the acidic condition, the amount of which was related to the concentration of MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni, Cu) MNPs. And the peroxidase-like catalytic behavior of MFe2O4MNPs was analyzed in detail. MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni, Cu) MNPs as the mimic peroxidase shows some advantages, such as easy preparation, stability, repeatability, dispersibility, and rapid separation over natural enzymes. The glucose biosensor showed good response with a linear range from9.4 /C2 10 /C0 7to2.5 /C2 10 /C0 5mol L /C0 1with a limit of detection (LOD) of4.5 /C2 10 /C0 7mol L /C0 1 , using NiFe2O4MNPs as a mimic peroxidase model. Furthermore, the proposed NiFe2O4MNPs-based system shows high selectivity and is capable of sensing in complicated biological samples such as urine sample. With further development, the MFe2O4 (M ¼ Mg, Ni, Cu) MNPs can be expected to be applied in variousfields, such as biotechnology, medical diagnostics and environmental monitoring.
